The church submitted a request for Ukrainian autocephaly at its founding synod in Kyiv in 1992.[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-154) These churches were competing with the [Ukrainian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_(Moscow_Patriarchate) "Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)") (UOC-MP), the Russian Church in Ukraine.[[154]](#cite_note-155)[[155]](#cite_note-156)

On 11 October 2018, the [Holy Synod of the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Holy_Synod_of_the_Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Holy Synod of the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") revoked the Russian Church's letter of issue, allowing them to ordain the Metropolitan of Kyiv, re-established a [stauropegion](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/stauropegion "wiktionary:stauropegion") in Kyiv, and lifted the Russian Church's excommunication of members of the UAOC and the UOC-KP. In response, on 15 October, the [Holy Synod of the Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Holy_Synod_of_the_Russian_Orthodox_Church "Holy Synod of the Russian Orthodox Church") severed all ties with the [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") and barred all members of the Russian Church from receiving communion or sacraments from any churches with ties to the Ecumenical Patriarchate.[[156]](#cite_note-157)[[157]](#cite_note-158) On 15 December 2018, the UAOC and UOC-KP voted to merge in the [Unification council of the Eastern Orthodox churches of Ukraine](/wiki/Unification_council_of_the_Eastern_Orthodox_churches_of_Ukraine "Unification council of the Eastern Orthodox churches of Ukraine"), forming the restored [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine"), with [Epiphanius I of Ukraine](/wiki/Epiphanius_I_of_Ukraine "Epiphanius I of Ukraine"), of the UOC-KP, becoming the first primate of the unified church.[[158]](#cite_note-159) On 5 January 2019, Bartholomew I signed the official [tomos](/wiki/Tomos_(Eastern_Orthodox_Church) "Tomos (Eastern Orthodox Church)") that granted [autocephaly](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") to the Orthodox Church of Ukraine.[[159]](#cite_note-160)

In addition to severing ties with the Ecumenical Patriarchate, the Russian Church has also severed communion with Archbishop [Ieronymos II of Athens](/wiki/Ieronymos_II_of_Athens "Ieronymos II of Athens") primate of the [Church of Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece"),[[160]](#cite_note-161)[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[162]](#cite_note-163) [Patriarch Theodore II](/wiki/Patriarch_Theodore_II_of_Alexandria "Patriarch Theodore II of Alexandria") [of Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria"),[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)[[165]](#cite_note-166)[[166]](#cite_note-167) and Archbishop [Chrysostomos II](/wiki/Chrysostomos_II_of_Cyprus "Chrysostomos II of Cyprus") of [Cyprus](/wiki/Church_of_Cyprus "Church of Cyprus").[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169)[[169]](#cite_note-170)[[170]](#cite_note-171) In response to the severing of ties with the Ecumenical Patriarchate, the [Archdiocese of Russian Orthodox Churches in Western Europe](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Russian_Orthodox_Churches_in_Western_Europe "Archdiocese of Russian Orthodox Churches in Western Europe") (AROCWE), voted to dissolve itself, although the vote failed, it resulted in a split in AROCWE, with several churches leaving to form the "Vicariate of Russian Tradition of the Metropolis of France", while [John (Renneteau)](/w/index.php?title=John_(Renneteau)&action=edit&redlink=1 "John (Renneteau) (page does not exist)") [[ru](https://ru.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D0%98%D0%BE%D0%B0%D0%BD%D0%BD_(%D0%A0%D0%B5%D0%BD%D0%BD%D0%B5%D1%82%D0%BE) "ru:Иоанн (Реннето)")], head of the AROCWE, personally joined the Russian Church.[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173)[[173]](#cite_note-174)[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[175]](#cite_note-176) After the [2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine"), the UOC-MP severed all ties with the Russian Church.[[176]](#cite_note-177)[[177]](#cite_note-178)[[178]](#cite_note-179)

## Organisation and leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Organisation and leadership")]

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox Church organization](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church_organization "Eastern Orthodox Church organization")

See also: [Canon law of the Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Canon_law_of_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Canon law of the Eastern Orthodox Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Timeline_of_the_History_of_the_main_autocephalous_Eastern_Orthodox_Churches%2C_E._Orthodox_point_of_view_%282022%29.svg/250px-Timeline_of_the_History_of_the_main_autocephalous_Eastern_Orthodox_Churches%2C_E._Orthodox_point_of_view_%282022%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Timeline_of_the_History_of_the_main_autocephalous_Eastern_Orthodox_Churches,_E._Orthodox_point_of_view_(2022).svg)

A timeline showing the main autocephalous Eastern Orthodox churches, from an Eastern Orthodox point of view, up to 2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Canonical_territories_of_autocephalous_and_autonomous_Eastern_Orthodox_jurisdictions_%282022%29.svg/250px-Canonical_territories_of_autocephalous_and_autonomous_Eastern_Orthodox_jurisdictions_%282022%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Canonical_territories_of_autocephalous_and_autonomous_Eastern_Orthodox_jurisdictions_(2022).svg)

The [canonical territories](/wiki/Canonical_territory "Canonical territory") of the main autocephalous and autonomous Eastern Orthodox jurisdictions as of 2022

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/St._Nicholas_Cathedral_%28Seoul%29_7.jpg/250px-St._Nicholas_Cathedral_%28Seoul%29_7.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Nicholas_Cathedral_(Seoul)_7.jpg)

[St. Nicholas Cathedral](/wiki/St._Nicholas_Cathedral_(Seoul) "St. Nicholas Cathedral (Seoul)") in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), Korea

The Eastern Orthodox Church is a fellowship of [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): αὐτοκέφαλος; "self-headed") churches, with the [ecumenical patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical patriarch of Constantinople") recognised as having *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* status. The patriarch of Constantinople has the honour of primacy, but his title is only *first among equals* and he has no real authority over churches other than the Constantinopolitan church. Rather, his role is limited to defined prerogatives interpreted by the ecumenical patriarch.[[179]](#cite_note-bbc-180)[[180]](#cite_note-181)[[181]](#cite_note-182) At times, though, the office of the ecumenical patriarch has been accused of Constantinopolitan or Eastern [papism](/wiki/Papism "Papism").[[182]](#cite_note-183)[[183]](#cite_note-184)[[184]](#cite_note-185)

The Eastern Orthodox Church considers [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") to be the head of the Church and the Church to be his body. It is believed that Church authority and the [grace of God](/wiki/Grace_of_God "Grace of God") is directly passed down to Orthodox [bishops](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") and other [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") through the [laying on of hands](/wiki/Laying_on_of_hands "Laying on of hands")—a practice started by the [New Testament apostles](/wiki/New_Testament_apostles "New Testament apostles")—and that this unbroken historical link is an essential element of the [true church](/wiki/One_true_church "One true church") ([Acts 8:17](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Acts%208:17&version=nrsv); [1 Timothy 4:14](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=1%20Timothy%204:14&version=nrsv); [Hebrews 6:2](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Hebrews%206:2&version=nrsv)) The Eastern Orthodox Church asserts that [apostolic succession](/wiki/Apostolic_succession "Apostolic succession") requires apostolic faith, and bishops without apostolic faith, who are in [heresy](/wiki/Heresy "Heresy"), forfeit their claim to apostolic succession.[[185]](#cite_note-186) Orthodox churches differentiate themselves from other Christian churches by practising "[ritual](/wiki/Ritual "Ritual") and [liturgy](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy")... rich in mystery and symbolism,"[[186]](#cite_note-RF-187) similar to their views on the sacraments.

The Eastern Orthodox communion is organised into several regional churches, which are either autocephalous or lower-ranking [autonomous](/wiki/Autonomy_(Eastern_Orthodoxy) "Autonomy (Eastern Orthodoxy)") ("self-governing") church bodies unified in [theology](/wiki/Theology "Theology") and worship. These include the fourteen autocephalous churches of the [Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople"), [Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Church of Alexandria"), [Antioch](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Antioch_and_All_the_East "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Antioch and All the East"), [Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Church_of_Cyprus "Church of Cyprus"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church"), [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), [Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece"), [Poland](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church"), [Romania](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church"), [Albania](/wiki/Albanian_Orthodox_Church "Albanian Orthodox Church"), and the [Czech Republic and Slovakia](/wiki/Czech_and_Slovak_Orthodox_Church "Czech and Slovak Orthodox Church"), which were officially invited to the [Pan-Orthodox Council](/wiki/Pan-Orthodox_Council "Pan-Orthodox Council") of 2016;[[187]](#cite_note-188) the [Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America") formed in 1970; the autocephalous [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") created in 2019; the [Macedonian Orthodox Church – Ohrid Archbishopric](/wiki/Macedonian_Orthodox_Church_%E2%80%93_Ohrid_Archbishopric "Macedonian Orthodox Church – Ohrid Archbishopric"), granted autocephaly by the Serbian Orthodox Church in 2022;[[188]](#cite_note-189) and a number of autonomous churches.[[179]](#cite_note-bbc-180) Each church has a ruling [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") and a [holy synod](/wiki/Holy_Synod "Holy Synod") to administer its jurisdiction and lead the Eastern Orthodox Church in the preservation and teaching of the apostolic and [patristic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/patristic "wikt:patristic") traditions and church practices.

Each bishop has a territory ([see](/wiki/Episcopal_see "Episcopal see")) over which he governs. His main duty is to make sure the traditions and practices of the Eastern Orthodox Church are preserved. Bishops are equal in authority and cannot interfere in the jurisdiction of another bishop. Administratively, these bishops and their territories are organised into various autocephalous groups or [synods](/wiki/Synod "Synod") of bishops who gather together at least twice each year to discuss the state of affairs within their respective sees. While bishops and their autocephalous synods have the ability to administer guidance in individual cases, their actions do not usually set precedents that affect the entire Eastern Orthodox Church. Bishops are almost always chosen from the monastic ranks and must remain unmarried.

### Church councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Church councils")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Rylands_Nicene_Creed_papyrus.jpg/250px-Rylands_Nicene_Creed_papyrus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rylands_Nicene_Creed_papyrus.jpg)

Oldest extant manuscript of the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed"), dated to the fifth century

The ecumenical councils followed a democratic form, with each bishop having one vote. Though present and allowed to speak before the council, members of the [Imperial Roman](/wiki/Imperial_Roman "Imperial Roman")/[Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") court, abbots, priests, deacons, monks and laymen were not allowed to vote. The primary goal of these great synods was to verify and confirm the fundamental beliefs of the [Great Christian Church](/wiki/Great_Church "Great Church") as truth, and to remove as heresy any false teachings that would threaten the Christian Church. The [pope of Rome](/wiki/Pope_of_Rome "Pope of Rome") at that time held the position of *[primus inter pares](/wiki/Primus_inter_pares "Primus inter pares")* ("first among equals") and, while he was not present at any of the councils, he continued to hold this title until the [East–West Schism](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_Schism "East–West Schism") of 1054.[[189]](#cite_note-190)[[190]](#cite_note-191)[[191]](#cite_note-192)[[192]](#cite_note-193)

Other councils have helped to define the Eastern Orthodox position, specifically the [Quinisext Council](/wiki/Quinisext_Council "Quinisext Council"), the Synods of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), [879–880](/wiki/Fourth_Council_of_Constantinople_(Eastern_Orthodox) "Fourth Council of Constantinople (Eastern Orthodox)"), [1341, 1347, 1351](/wiki/Fifth_Council_of_Constantinople "Fifth Council of Constantinople"), 1583, 1819, and [1872](/wiki/Phyletism "Phyletism"), the [Synod of Iași](/wiki/Synod_of_Ia%C8%99i "Synod of Iași"), 1642, and the Pan-Orthodox [Synod of Jerusalem](/wiki/Synod_of_Jerusalem_(1672) "Synod of Jerusalem (1672)"), 1672; the [Pan-Orthodox Council](/wiki/Pan-Orthodox_Council "Pan-Orthodox Council"), held in Greece in 2016, was the only such Eastern Orthodox council in modern times.

According to Eastern Orthodox teaching the position of "first among equals" gives no additional power or authority to the bishop that holds it, but rather that this person sits as organisational head of a council of equals (like a president).[[193]](#cite_note-194)

One of the decisions made by the [First Council of Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") (the second ecumenical council, meeting in 381) and supported by later such councils was that the Patriarch of Constantinople should be given equal honour to the Pope of Rome since Constantinople was considered to be the "[New Rome](/wiki/New_Rome "New Rome")". According to the third [canon](/wiki/Canon_(canon_law) "Canon (canon law)") of the second ecumenical council: "Because [Constantinople] is new Rome, the bishop of Constantinople is to enjoy the privileges of honor after the bishop of Rome".[[194]](#cite_note-195)

The 28th canon of the fourth ecumenical council clarified this point by stating: "For the Fathers rightly granted privileges to the throne of Old Rome because it was the royal city. And the One Hundred and Fifty most religious Bishops (i.e. the second ecumenical council in 381) actuated by the same consideration, gave equal privileges to the most holy throne of New Rome, justly judging that the city which is honoured with the Sovereignty and the Senate, and enjoys equal privileges with the old imperial Rome, should in ecclesiastical matters also be magnified as she is."[[195]](#cite_note-196)

Because of the schism, the Eastern Orthodox no longer recognise the primacy of the pope of Rome. The patriarch of Constantinople therefore, like the Pope before him, now enjoys the title of "first among equals".

### Adherents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Adherents")]

Further information: [Eastern Orthodoxy by country](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_by_country "Eastern Orthodoxy by country")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_World_%28ARDA_2015%29.png/500px-Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_World_%28ARDA_2015%29.png)](/wiki/File:Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_World_(ARDA_2015).png)

Percentage distribution of Eastern Orthodox Christians by country, 2015[[196]](#cite_note-arda-197)

The most reliable estimates currently available number Eastern Orthodox adherents at around 300 million,[[197]](#cite_note-198)[[198]](#cite_note-199)[[199]](#cite_note-200)[[200]](#cite_note-Peworthodox-201)[[201]](#cite_note-CSGC-2019-202) making Eastern Orthodoxy the second largest Christian [communion](/wiki/Koinonia "Koinonia") in the world after the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[202]](#cite_note-203)[[b]](#cite_note-205)

According to the 2015 Yearbook of International Religious Demography, as of 2010, the Eastern Orthodox population was 4% of the global population, declining from 7.1% in 1910. The study also found a decrease in proportional terms, with Eastern Orthodox Christians making up 12.2% of the world's total Christian population in 2015 compared to 20.4% a century earlier.[[204]](#cite_note-Yearbook-206) A 2017 report by the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") reached similar figures, noting that Eastern Orthodoxy has seen slower growth and less geographic spread than Catholicism and Protestantism, which were driven by [colonialism](/wiki/Colonialism "Colonialism") and [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") activity across the world.[[205]](#cite_note-207)

Over two-thirds of all Eastern Orthodox members are concentrated in [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"), [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Russia](/wiki/Asian_Russia "Asian Russia"),[[206]](#cite_note-208) with significant minorities in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") and the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant"). However, Eastern Orthodoxy has become more globalised over the last century, seeing greater growth in [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe"), [the Americas](/wiki/New_World "New World"), and parts of Africa; churches are present in the major cities of most countries.[[207]](#cite_note-209) Adherents constitute the largest single religious [community](/wiki/Faith "Faith") in Russia[[208]](#cite_note-Sreda2012-210)[[c]](#cite_note-213)—which is home to roughly half the world's Eastern Orthodox Christians—and are the majority in [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212)[[211]](#cite_note-Razumkov2006-214) [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Belarus](/wiki/Belarus "Belarus"),[[212]](#cite_note-Belarus2011-215) [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"),[[d]](#cite_note-216)[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [North Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia "North Macedonia"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) and [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro");[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) communities also dominate the disputed territories of [Abkhazia](/wiki/Abkhazia "Abkhazia"), [South Ossetia](/wiki/South_Ossetia "South Ossetia") and [Transnistria](/wiki/Transnistria "Transnistria"). Significant Eastern Orthodox minorities exist in [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"),[[e]](#cite_note-217)[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"),[[213]](#cite_note-Tieslietu-218) [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"),[[214]](#cite_note-219) [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"),[[215]](#cite_note-220) [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"),[[216]](#cite_note-221) [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"),[[217]](#cite_note-freedom-222) [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"),[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212) and many other countries.

Eastern Orthodox Christianity is the fastest growing religion in certain Western countries, primarily through labour migration from Eastern Europe, and to a lesser degree conversion.[[218]](#cite_note-Hämmerli-223) Ireland saw a doubling of its Eastern Orthodox population between 2006 and 2011.[[218]](#cite_note-Hämmerli-223)[[219]](#cite_note-224)[[220]](#cite_note-225) Spain and Germany have the [largest communities in Western Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_by_country "Eastern Orthodoxy by country"), at roughly 1.5 million each,[[221]](#cite_note-226)[[222]](#cite_note-227)
followed by Italy with around 900,000 and France with between 500,000 and 700,000.

In the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), four countries have over 100,000 Eastern Orthodox Christians: Canada, Mexico, Brazil, and the United States; all but the latter had fewer than 20,000 at the turn of the 20th century.[[223]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-228) The U.S. has seen its community more than quadruple since 1910, from 460,000 to 1.8 million as of 2017;[[223]](#cite_note-pewforum.org-228) consequently, the number of Eastern Orthodox parishes has been growing, with a 16% increase between 2000 and 2010.[[224]](#cite_note-Jones2010-229)[[f]](#cite_note-231)[[g]](#cite_note-234)

Turkey, which for centuries once had one of the largest Eastern Orthodox communities, saw its overall Christian population fall from roughly one-fifth in 1914 to 2.5% in 1927.[[228]](#cite_note-235) This was predominantly due to the [dissolution of the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Ottoman_Empire "Dissolution of the Ottoman Empire"), which saw most Christian territories become independent nations. The remaining Christian population was reduced further by large-scale genocides against the [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"), [Greek](/wiki/Greek_genocide "Greek genocide"), [Assyrian communities](/wiki/Assyrian_genocide "Assyrian genocide"); subsequent [population exchanges between Greece and Turkey](/wiki/Population_exchanges_between_Greece_and_Turkey "Population exchanges between Greece and Turkey")[[229]](#cite_note-236) and Bulgaria and Turkey; and associated [emigration of Christians](/wiki/Christian_emigration "Christian emigration") to foreign countries (mostly in Europe and [the Americas](/wiki/The_Americas "The Americas")).[[230]](#cite_note-237) Today, only 0.2% of Turkey's population represent [various Christian denominations](/wiki/Christianity_in_Turkey "Christianity in Turkey") (320,000).[[231]](#cite_note-238)[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212)

## Theology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Theology")]

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox Christian theology](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Christian_theology "Eastern Orthodox Christian theology")

### Trinity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Trinity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Andrey_Rublev_-_%D0%A1%D0%B2._%D0%A2%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%86%D0%B0_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Andrey_Rublev_-_%D0%A1%D0%B2._%D0%A2%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%86%D0%B0_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrey_Rublev_-_%D0%A1%D0%B2._%D0%A2%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B8%D1%86%D0%B0_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[The Trinity](/wiki/Trinity_(Andrei_Rublev) "Trinity (Andrei Rublev)")* by Russian icon painter [Andrei Rublev](/wiki/Andrei_Rublev "Andrei Rublev"), early 15th century

Eastern Orthodox Christians believe in the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), three distinct, divine persons (*[hypostases](/wiki/Hypostasis_(philosophy) "Hypostasis (philosophy)")*), without overlap or [modality](/wiki/Sabellianism "Sabellianism") among them, who each have the same divine [essence](/wiki/Essence "Essence") (*ousia*, Greek: οὐσία)—uncreated, immaterial, and [eternal](/wiki/God_and_eternity "God and eternity").[[253]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993208–211-260) These three persons are typically distinguished by their relation to each other. The [Father](/wiki/God_the_Father "God the Father") is eternal and not begotten and does not proceed from any, the [Son](/wiki/God_the_Son "God the Son") is eternal and begotten of the Father, and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") is eternal and proceeds from the Father.[[254]](#cite_note-Theokritoff_2010-261) Orthodox doctrine regarding the Trinity is summarised in the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed#Niceno-Constantinopolitan_Creed "Nicene Creed").[[255]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993202-262)

Eastern Orthodox Christians believe in a [monotheistic](/wiki/Monotheistic "Monotheistic") [conception of God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") (God is only one), which is both [transcendent](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)") (wholly independent of, and removed from, the material universe) and [immanent](/wiki/Immanent "Immanent") (involved in the material universe).[[254]](#cite_note-Theokritoff_2010-261)

In discussing God's relationship to his creation, Eastern Orthodox theology [distinguishes](/wiki/Essence%E2%80%93Energies_distinction "Essence–Energies distinction") between God's eternal essence, which is totally transcendent, and his *uncreated energies*, which is how he reaches humanity.[[254]](#cite_note-Theokritoff_2010-261) The God who is transcendent and the God who touches mankind are one and the same.[[254]](#cite_note-Theokritoff_2010-261) That is, these energies are not something that proceed from God or that God produces, but rather they are God himself: distinct, yet inseparable from God's inner being.[[256]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare199367–69-263) This view is often called [Palamism](/wiki/Palamism "Palamism").

In understanding the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity") as "one God in three persons", "three persons" is not to be emphasised more than "one God", and vice versa. While the three persons are distinct, they are united in one divine essence, and their oneness is expressed in community and action so completely that they cannot be considered separately. For example, their salvation of mankind is an activity engaged in common: "Christ became man by the good will of the Father and by the cooperation of the Holy Spirit. Christ sends the Holy Spirit who proceeds from the Father, and the Holy Spirit forms Christ in our hearts, and thus God the Father is glorified." Their "communion of essence" is "indivisible". Trinitarian terminology—essence, hypostasis, etc.—are used "philosophically", "to answer the ideas of the heretics", and "to place the terms where they separate error and truth."[[257]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHierotheos1998128–130-264) The words do what they can do, but the nature of the Trinity in its fullness is believed to remain beyond man's comprehension and expression, a holy mystery that can only be experienced.

### Sin, salvation, and the incarnation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Sin, salvation, and the incarnation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Hosios_Loukas_%28narthex%29_-_East_wall%2C_right_%28Harrowing_of_Hell%29_03.jpg/330px-Hosios_Loukas_%28narthex%29_-_East_wall%2C_right_%28Harrowing_of_Hell%29_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hosios_Loukas_(narthex)_-_East_wall,_right_(Harrowing_of_Hell)_03.jpg)

[Harrowing of Hell](/wiki/Harrowing_of_Hell "Harrowing of Hell"), mosaic in the Monastery of [Osios Loukas](/wiki/Osios_Loukas "Osios Loukas"), 11th century

When Eastern Orthodox Christians refer to fallen nature they are not saying that human nature has become evil in itself. Human nature is still formed in the image of God; humans are still God's creation, and God has never created anything evil, but fallen nature remains open to evil intents and actions. It is sometimes said among Eastern Orthodox that humans are "inclined to sin"; that is, people find some sinful things attractive. It is the nature of temptation to make sinful things seem the more attractive, and it is the fallen nature of humans that seeks or succumbs to the attraction. Orthodox Christians reject the [Augustinian position](/wiki/Augustinianism "Augustinianism") that the descendants of Adam and Eve are actually guilty of the original sin of their ancestors.[[258]](#cite_note-265)

Since the fall of man, then, it has been mankind's dilemma that no human can restore his nature to union with God's grace; it was necessary for God to effect another change in human nature. Eastern Orthodox Christians believe that Christ Jesus was both God and Man absolutely and completely, having two natures indivisibly: eternally begotten of the Father in his divinity, he was born in his humanity of a woman, Mary, by her consent, through descent of the Holy Spirit. He lived on earth, in time and history, as a man. As a man he also died, and went to the place of the dead, which is [Hades](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hades "Christian views on Hades"). But being God, neither death nor Hades could contain him, and he rose to life again, in his humanity, by the power of the Holy Spirit, thus destroying the power of Hades and of death itself.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChrysostom400[[Paschal_Homily]]-266)

Through Christ's destruction of Hades' power to hold humanity hostage, he made the path to salvation effective for all the righteous who had died from the beginning of time—saving many, including Adam and Eve, who are remembered in the church as saints.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESt._Athanasius1982Ch._2–3,_p._318-267)

### Resurrection of Christ

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Resurrection of Christ")]

The Eastern Orthodox Church understands the death and resurrection of Jesus to be real historical events, as described in the gospels of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament").

### Christian life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Christian life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Meteora_-_Rousanou_Monastery_1.jpg/250px-Meteora_-_Rousanou_Monastery_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meteora_-_Rousanou_Monastery_1.jpg)

[Monastery of Rousanou](/wiki/Monastery_of_Rousanou "Monastery of Rousanou") in Greece.

Church teaching is that Eastern Orthodox Christians, through baptism, enter a new life of salvation through repentance whose purpose is to share in the life of God through the work of the Holy Spirit. The Eastern Orthodox Christian life is a spiritual pilgrimage in which each person, through the [imitation of Christ](/wiki/Imitation_of_Christ "Imitation of Christ") and *[hesychasm](/wiki/Hesychasm "Hesychasm")*,[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHierotheos1998234–237,_(241=Glossary)-268) cultivates the practice of unceasing prayer. Each life occurs within the life of the church as a member of the [body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ").[[262]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorge2006[httporthodoxinfocomgeneraltheosis-englishpdf_34]-269) It is then through the fire of God's love in the action of the Holy Spirit that each member becomes more holy, more wholly unified with Christ, starting in this life and continuing in the next.[[263]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOxford_Dict_Christian_Church3rd_ed-270)[[264]](#cite_note-271) The church teaches that everyone, being born in God's image, is called to [theosis](/wiki/Theosis_(Eastern_Christian_theology) "Theosis (Eastern Christian theology)"), fulfilment of the image in likeness to God. God the creator, having divinity by nature, offers each person participation in divinity by cooperatively accepting His gift of grace.[[265]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGeorge2006[httporthodoxinfocomgeneraltheosis-englishpdf_21]-272)

The Eastern Orthodox Church, in understanding itself to be the [Body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ"), and similarly in understanding the Christian life to lead to the unification in Christ of all members of his body, views the church as embracing all Christ's members, those now living on earth, and also all those through the ages who have passed on to the heavenly life. "In general," Eastern Orthodox Christianity sees the Church "as a purely mystical body, the understanding of which cannot be attained through the development of a rational or [natural theology](/wiki/Natural_theology "Natural theology")."[[186]](#cite_note-RF-187)

The church includes the Christian saints from all times, and also judges, prophets and righteous Jews of the first covenant, Adam and Eve, even the angels and heavenly hosts.[[266]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHierotheos199825–30-273) In Eastern Orthodox services, the earthly members together with the heavenly members worship God as one community in Christ, in a union that transcends time and space and joins heaven to earth. This unity of the church is sometimes called the *communion of the saints*.[[267]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHierotheos199823-274)

#### Eastern Orthodox Order of Saint Benedict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Eastern Orthodox Order of Saint Benedict")]

The Order of Saint Benedict is an affiliation of monastics of the Eastern Orthodox Church who strive to live according to the [Rule of St Benedict](/wiki/Rule_of_St_Benedict "Rule of St Benedict"). The equivalent monastic order in the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is known as the [Order of Saint Benedict](/wiki/Order_of_Saint_Benedict "Order of Saint Benedict"), abbreviated as OSB.

Within the United States, the [Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of North America](/wiki/Antiochian_Orthodox_Archdiocese_of_North_America "Antiochian Orthodox Archdiocese of North America") has at least one Benedictine monastery.[[268]](#cite_note-275)

Several Benedictine monastic houses, *[sketes](/wiki/Skete "Skete")* and hermitages fit within the Russian Orthodox Church outside of Russia, all of which are [*stavropegial*](/wiki/Stauropegic_monastery "Stauropegic monastery") directly under the Metropolitan. An oblate programme exists for Orthodox laity Saint Benedict Russian Orthodox Church in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma.[[269]](#cite_note-276)

### Virgin Mary and other saints

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Virgin Mary and other saints")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Virgin_of_Vladimir.jpg/250px-Virgin_of_Vladimir.jpg)](/wiki/File:Virgin_of_Vladimir.jpg)

The [Theotokos of Vladimir](/wiki/Theotokos_of_Vladimir "Theotokos of Vladimir"), one of the most venerated of Orthodox Christian icons of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Johndamascene.jpg/250px-Johndamascene.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johndamascene.jpg)

[John of Damascus](/wiki/John_of_Damascus "John of Damascus")

The Eastern Orthodox Church believes death and the separation of body and soul to be unnatural—a result of the [Fall of Man](/wiki/Fall_of_Man "Fall of Man"). They also hold that the congregation of the church comprises both the living and the dead. All persons currently in heaven are considered to be [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), whether their names are known or not. There are, however, those saints of distinction whom God has revealed as particularly good examples. When a saint is revealed and ultimately recognised by a large portion of the church a service of official recognition ([glorification](/wiki/Glorification "Glorification")) is celebrated.[[270]](#cite_note-277)

This does not "make" the person a saint; it merely recognises the fact and announces it to the rest of the church. A day is prescribed for the saint's celebration, hymns composed and icons created. Numerous saints are celebrated on each day of the year. They are venerated (shown great respect and love) but not worshipped, for worship is due God alone (this view is also held by the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy") and [Catholic churches](/wiki/Catholic_church "Catholic church")). In showing the saints this love and requesting their prayers, the Eastern Orthodox manifest their belief that the saints thus assist in the process of salvation for others.

Pre-eminent among the saints is the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") (commonly referred to as *Theotokos* or *Bogoroditsa*: "[Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God")"). In [Eastern Orthodox theology](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_theology "Eastern Orthodox theology"), the Mother of God is the fulfilment of the Old Testament archetypes revealed in the [Ark of the Covenant](/wiki/Ark_of_the_Covenant "Ark of the Covenant") (because she carried the New Covenant in the person of Christ) and the [burning bush](/wiki/Burning_bush "Burning bush") that appeared before [Moses](/wiki/Moses "Moses") (symbolising the Mother of God's carrying of God without being consumed).[[271]](#cite_note-278)

The Eastern Orthodox believe that Christ, from the moment of his conception, was both fully God and fully human. Mary is thus called the *Theotokos* or *Bogoroditsa* as an affirmation of the divinity of the one to whom she gave birth. It is also believed that her virginity was not compromised in conceiving God-incarnate, that she was not harmed and that she remained forever a virgin. Scriptural references to "brothers" of Christ are interpreted as kin, given that the word "brother" was used in multiple ways, as was the term "father". Due to her unique place in salvation history, Mary is honoured above all other saints and especially venerated for the great work that God accomplished through her.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993257–258-279)

The Eastern Orthodox Church regards the bodies of all saints as holy, made such by participation in the holy mysteries, especially the communion of Christ's holy body and blood, and by the indwelling of the Holy Spirit within the church. Indeed, that persons and physical things can be made holy is a cornerstone of the doctrine of the [Incarnation](/wiki/Incarnation_(Christianity) "Incarnation (Christianity)"), made manifest also directly by God in Old Testament times through his dwelling in the Ark of the Covenant. Thus, physical items connected with saints are also regarded as holy, through their participation in the earthly works of those saints. According to church teaching and tradition, God himself bears witness to this holiness of saints' [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") through the many miracles connected with them that have been reported throughout history since biblical times, often including healing from disease and injury.[[273]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993234-280)

### Eschatology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Eschatology")]

Main article: [Christian eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Mosaic_of_the_Last_Judgment_of_Santa_Maria_Assunta_%28Torcello%291.jpg/250px-Mosaic_of_the_Last_Judgment_of_Santa_Maria_Assunta_%28Torcello%291.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mosaic_of_the_Last_Judgment_of_Santa_Maria_Assunta_(Torcello)1.jpg)

*Last Judgment*: 12th-century [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") mosaic from [Torcello](/wiki/Torcello "Torcello") Cathedral

Orthodox Christians believe that when a person dies the soul is temporarily separated from the body. Though it may linger for a short period on Earth, it is ultimately escorted either to paradise ([Abraham's bosom](/wiki/Abraham%27s_bosom "Abraham's bosom")) or the darkness of [Hades](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hades "Christian views on Hades"), following the [Temporary Judgment](/wiki/Particular_judgment "Particular judgment"). Orthodox do not accept the doctrine of [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"), which is held by Catholicism. The soul's experience of either of these states is only a "foretaste"—being experienced only by the soul—until the [Final Judgment](/wiki/Final_Judgment "Final Judgment"), when the soul and body will be reunited.[[274]](#cite_note-281)[[275]](#cite_note-soul-282)

The Eastern Orthodox believe that the state of the soul in Hades can be affected by the love and prayers of the righteous up until the Last Judgment.[[276]](#cite_note-283) For this reason the church offers a special [prayer for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead") on the third day, ninth day, fortieth day, and the one-year anniversary after the death of an Orthodox Christian. There are also several days throughout the year that are set aside for general commemoration of the departed, sometimes including nonbelievers. These days usually fall on a Saturday, since it was on a Saturday that Christ lay in the [Tomb](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Sepulchre "Church of the Holy Sepulchre").[[275]](#cite_note-soul-282)

The Eastern Orthodox believe that after the Final Judgment:

* All souls will be reunited with their [resurrected bodies](/wiki/Resurrection_of_the_Dead "Resurrection of the Dead").
* All souls will fully experience their spiritual state.
* Having been perfected, the saints will forever progress towards a deeper and fuller love of God, which equates with eternal happiness.[[275]](#cite_note-soul-282)

### Bible

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Bible")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Letter_of_Eusebius_to_Karpianis._Alaverdi_Gospels%2C_A-484%2C_1%2C_1054.jpg/250px-Letter_of_Eusebius_to_Karpianis._Alaverdi_Gospels%2C_A-484%2C_1%2C_1054.jpg)](/wiki/File:Letter_of_Eusebius_to_Karpianis._Alaverdi_Gospels,_A-484,_1,_1054.jpg)

[Alaverdi Gospels](/wiki/Alaverdi_Gospels "Alaverdi Gospels"), an 11th-century [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_language "Georgian language") [illuminated manuscript](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") [Gospel Book](/wiki/Gospel_Book "Gospel Book")

The official Bible of the Eastern Orthodox Church contains the [Septuagint](/wiki/Septuagint "Septuagint") text of the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament"), with the [Book of Daniel](/wiki/Book_of_Daniel "Book of Daniel") given in the translation by [Theodotion](/wiki/Theodotion "Theodotion"). The [Patriarchal Text](/wiki/Patriarchal_Text "Patriarchal Text") is used for the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament").[[277]](#cite_note-284)[[278]](#cite_note-285) Orthodox Christians hold that the Bible is a verbal icon of Christ, as proclaimed by the [7th ecumenical council](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Nicaea "Second Council of Nicaea").[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1991209-286) They refer to the Bible as [holy scripture](/wiki/Holy_scripture "Holy scripture"), meaning writings containing the foundational truths of the Christian faith as revealed by Christ and the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") to its divinely inspired human authors. Holy scripture forms the primary and authoritative written witness of [holy tradition](/wiki/Holy_tradition "Holy tradition") and is essential as the basis for all Orthodox teaching and belief.[[280]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1991209_(quoting_[[John_Chrysostom]]):_"It_is_impossible_for_a_man_to_be_saved_if_he_does_not_read_the_Scriptures."-287)

Once established as holy scripture, there has never been any question that the Eastern Orthodox Church holds the full list of books to be venerable and beneficial for reading and study,[[281]](#cite_note-288) even though it informally holds some books in higher esteem than others, the four gospels highest of all. Of the subgroups significant enough to be named, the "[Anagignoskomena](/wiki/Anagignoskomena "Anagignoskomena")" (ἀναγιγνωσκόμενα, "things that are read") comprises ten of the Old Testament books rejected in the [Protestant canon](/wiki/Protestant_canon "Protestant canon"),[[282]](#cite_note-289) but deemed by the Eastern Orthodox worthy to be read in worship services, even though they carry a lesser esteem than the 39 books of the [Hebrew canon](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible").[[283]](#cite_note-290) The lowest tier contains the remaining books not accepted by either Protestants or Catholics, among them, [Psalm 151](/wiki/Psalm_151 "Psalm 151"). Though it is a psalm, and is in the book of psalms, it is not classified as being within the Psalter (the first 150 psalms).[[284]](#cite_note-291)

In a very strict sense, it is not entirely orthodox to call the holy scripture the "Word of God". That is a title the Eastern Orthodox Church reserves for Christ, as supported in the scriptures themselves, most explicitly in the [first chapter of the Gospel of John](/wiki/John_1 "John 1"). God's Word is not hollow, like human words. "God said, 'let there be light'; and there was light."[[285]](#cite_note-292)

The Eastern Orthodox Church does not subscribe to the Protestant doctrine of *[sola scriptura](/wiki/Sola_scriptura "Sola scriptura")*. The church has defined what Scripture is; it also interprets what its meaning is.[[286]](#cite_note-293) Christ promised: "When He, the Spirit of truth, has come, He will guide you into all truth".[[287]](#cite_note-294)

Scriptures are understood to contain historical fact, poetry, idiom, metaphor, simile, moral fable, parable, prophecy and [wisdom literature](/wiki/Wisdom_literature "Wisdom literature"), and each bears its own consideration in its interpretation. While divinely inspired, the text still consists of words in human languages, arranged in humanly recognisable forms. The Eastern Orthodox Church does not oppose honest critical and historical study of the Bible.[[288]](#cite_note-Ware1991-295)

## Liturgy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Liturgy")]

Main articles: [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite"), [Eastern Orthodox worship](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_worship "Eastern Orthodox worship"), and [Western Rite Orthodoxy](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Meister_der_Palastkapelle_in_Palermo_003.jpg/250px-Meister_der_Palastkapelle_in_Palermo_003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Meister_der_Palastkapelle_in_Palermo_003.jpg)

[Icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon") of Ss. [Basil the Great](/wiki/Basil_the_Great "Basil the Great") (left) and [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom"), ascribed authors of the two most frequently used Eastern Orthodox Divine Liturgies, c. 1150 ([mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") in the [Palatine Chapel](/wiki/Cappella_Palatina "Cappella Palatina"), [Palermo](/wiki/Palermo "Palermo")).

### Church calendar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Church calendar")]

Lesser cycles also run in tandem with the annual ones. A weekly cycle of days prescribes a specific focus for each day in addition to others that may be observed.[[289]](#cite_note-296)

> Each day of the Weekly Cycle is dedicated to certain special memorials. Sunday is dedicated to [Christ's Resurrection](/wiki/Christ%27s_Resurrection "Christ's Resurrection"); Monday honours the holy bodiless powers (angels, archangels, etc.); Tuesday is dedicated to the prophets and especially the greatest of the prophets, [John the Forerunner and Baptist of the Lord](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"); Wednesday is consecrated to the Cross and recalls Judas' betrayal; Thursday honours the holy apostles and hierarchs, especially [Nicholas, Bishop of Myra in Lycia](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"); Friday is also consecrated to the Cross and recalls the day of the Crucifixion; Saturday is dedicated to All Saints, especially the [Mother of God](/wiki/Mother_of_God "Mother of God"), and to the memory of all those who have departed this life in the hope of resurrection and eternal life.

### Church services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Church services")]

See also: [Canonical hours § Byzantine Rite usage](/wiki/Canonical_hours#Byzantine_Rite_usage "Canonical hours")

The main service offered in the church is the [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy"). Most parishes offer this service on Sunday mornings and on major feast days, though it can be offered almost any day of the year.[[290]](#cite_note-297) Additional services include [Orthros](/wiki/Orthros "Orthros") and [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers"); prayer services in the morning and evening, respectively. The celebration of the feasts are distinguished according to their various degrees of solemnity. The great feasts are celebrated with an All Night Vigil. Lesser feasts will have a vigil according to the feast's custom.[[291]](#cite_note-CS-298)

Church service books are used in divine services, like the Gospel, the Epistle, and the Psalter. These books, often called *divine service books*, were composed in accordance with the scriptures and traditions of the Church Fathers and teachers of the Orthodox Church. During fasting, in the middle of the week Holy Communion is given at all church services, without removing the characteristics of Orthodox Lent.[[291]](#cite_note-CS-298)

#### Chanting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Chanting")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Patriarchate_Constantinopolis.jpg/250px-Patriarchate_Constantinopolis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Patriarchate_Constantinopolis.jpg)

Chanters singing on the [kliros](/wiki/Kliros "Kliros") at the [Church of St. George](/wiki/Church_of_St._George,_Istanbul "Church of St. George, Istanbul"), [Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Patriarchate of Constantinople")

Chanting is not considered "music" by Orthodox Christians, but rather sacred melody and prayer. An [Orthodox Divine Service](/wiki/Divine_Service_(Eastern_Orthodoxy) "Divine Service (Eastern Orthodoxy)") is chanted in its entirely by the clergy, the choir, and the congregation from the start of the service to the end.[[144]](#cite_note-HD-144) Early Christian forms of chanting began with ancient Judaic traditions of chanting the Psalms. As the Church grew, many new forms of hymns began to appear.[[292]](#cite_note-299)

Liturgies contain the reading and chanting of prayers, headed by a bishop or priest.[[291]](#cite_note-CS-298) Various liturgies include different forms of chanting services (for example, the [Liturgy of St. Basil the Great](/wiki/Liturgy_of_Saint_Basil "Liturgy of Saint Basil") has chanting of longer duration and the priest privately reads his prayers at the altar). For the composition of religious chant, the [Octoechos](/wiki/Octoechos "Octoechos"), an eight-[tone (mode)](/wiki/Mode_(music) "Mode (music)") system, analogous to the [Gregorian modes](/wiki/Gregorian_mode "Gregorian mode") in the West, and to other ancient Christian musical systems, is used. [Byzantine music](/wiki/Byzantine_music "Byzantine music") is [microtonal](/wiki/Microtonal_music "Microtonal music").

Byzantine chants are associated with the Eastern Roman Empire's period (AD 330 to 1453) and developed from Jewish and Syrian traditions in the early Christian Church. This continued to evolve until the 16th century.[[144]](#cite_note-HD-144)

Northern Slav have used simpler tonal systems evolved through the local types of [Znamenny chant](/wiki/Znamenny_chant "Znamenny chant"); today [Western music](/wiki/Western_culture#Music "Western culture"), often with [four-part harmony](/wiki/Four-part_harmony "Four-part harmony"), and the "tones" are simply sets of melodies. Russian liturgical chants (including those of some Ukrainian and Balkan churches) evolved from the Kievan Rus people after AD 988. Byzantine melodies adapted to the patterns of the [Old Church Slavonic](/wiki/Old_Church_Slavonic "Old Church Slavonic") language. In the 14th century, Russian elements began to be used in the church. By the 16th century, Russian chants had many links to the Byzantine style.[[144]](#cite_note-HD-144)

There are numerous versions and styles that are traditional and accepted and these vary a great deal between cultures.[[293]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993238-300)

## Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Traditions")]

### Art and architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Art and architecture")]

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox church architecture](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_church_architecture "Eastern Orthodox church architecture")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Orthodox-Church-interior.jpg/330px-Orthodox-Church-interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Orthodox-Church-interior.jpg)

An illustration of the traditional interior of an Orthodox church

The [Archdiocesan Cathedral of the Holy Trinity](/wiki/Archdiocesan_Cathedral_of_the_Holy_Trinity "Archdiocesan Cathedral of the Holy Trinity") on New York City's [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") is the largest Orthodox Christian church in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere").[[294]](#cite_note-StudentEd-301)

### Icons and symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Icons and symbols")]

See also: [Christian symbolism](/wiki/Christian_symbolism "Christian symbolism")

#### Icons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Icons")]

Further information: [Icon](/wiki/Icon "Icon")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Ushakov_Nerukotvorniy.jpg/250px-Ushakov_Nerukotvorniy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ushakov_Nerukotvorniy.jpg)

*Image of the Saviour [Not Made by Hand](/wiki/Acheiropoieta "Acheiropoieta")*: a traditional Orthodox [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") in the interpretation of [Simon Ushakov](/wiki/Simon_Ushakov "Simon Ushakov") (1658).

Aspects of the [iconography](/wiki/Iconography "Iconography") borrow from the pre-Christian [Roman](/wiki/Roman_art "Roman art") and [Hellenistic art](/wiki/Hellenistic_art "Hellenistic art"). [Henry Chadwick](/wiki/Henry_Chadwick_(theologian) "Henry Chadwick (theologian)") wrote, "In this instinct there was a measure of truth. The representations of Christ as the Almighty Lord on his judgment throne owed something to pictures of Zeus. Portraits of the Mother of God were not wholly independent of a pagan past of venerated mother-goddesses. In the popular mind the saints had come to fill a role that had been played by heroes and deities."[[295]](#cite_note-302)

Icons can be found adorning the walls of churches and often cover the inside structure completely.[[296]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993271-303) Most Eastern Orthodox homes have an area set aside for family prayer, usually an eastern facing wall, where are hung many icons. Icons have been part of Orthodox Christianity since the beginning of the church.[[297]](#cite_note-304)

#### Iconostasis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Iconostasis")]

Main article: [Iconostasis](/wiki/Iconostasis "Iconostasis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Iconostasis_%28People%27s_Salvation_Cathedral_-_Bucharest%29.jpg/250px-Iconostasis_%28People%27s_Salvation_Cathedral_-_Bucharest%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iconostasis_(People%27s_Salvation_Cathedral_-_Bucharest).jpg)

Iconostasis of the [Romanian People's Salvation Cathedral](/wiki/Romanian_People%27s_Salvation_Cathedral "Romanian People's Salvation Cathedral")

An *iconostasis*, also called the *templon*, is a wall of [icons](/wiki/Icons "Icons") and religious paintings, separating the [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") from the [sanctuary](/wiki/Sanctuary "Sanctuary") in a [church](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)"). *Iconostasis* also refers to a portable icon stand that can be placed anywhere within a church. The modern iconostasis evolved from the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_architecture "Byzantine architecture") [templon](/wiki/Templon "Templon") in the 11th century. The evolution of the iconostasis probably owes a great deal to 14th-century [Hesychast](/wiki/Hesychasm "Hesychasm") [mysticism](/wiki/Mysticism "Mysticism") and the wood-carving genius of the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church").

The first ceiling-high, five-leveled Russian iconostasis was designed by [Andrey Rublyov](/wiki/Andrey_Rublyov "Andrey Rublyov") in the [cathedral of the Dormition](/wiki/Cathedral_of_the_Dormition "Cathedral of the Dormition") in [Vladimir](/wiki/Vladimir,_Russia "Vladimir, Russia") in 1408.

#### Cross

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Cross")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Greek_cross.svg/120px-Greek_cross.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Greek_cross.svg)

[Greek cross](/wiki/Greek_Cross "Greek Cross")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Symbol_of_Orthodoxy.svg/120px-Symbol_of_Orthodoxy.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Symbol_of_Orthodoxy.svg)

[Orthodox cross](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_cross "Russian Orthodox cross")

On the Russian Orthodox cross, the small top crossbar represents the sign that [Pontius Pilate](/wiki/Pontius_Pilate "Pontius Pilate") nailed above Christ's head. It often is inscribed with an acronym, "INRI", [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Iesus Nazarenus, Rex Iudaeorum* for "[Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews](/wiki/INRI "INRI")" or "INBI", [Koine Greek](/wiki/Koine_Greek "Koine Greek"): Ἰησοῦς ὁ Ναζωραῖος ὁ βασιλεύς τῶν Ἰουδαίων for "Jesus of Nazareth, King of the Jews".[[298]](#cite_note-305)

Other crosses associated with the Eastern Orthodox Church are the more traditional single-bar crosses, budded designs, the [Greek cross](/wiki/Greek_cross "Greek cross"), the [Latin cross](/wiki/Latin_cross "Latin cross"), the [Jerusalem cross](/wiki/Jerusalem_cross "Jerusalem cross") (cross pattée), [Celtic crosses](/wiki/Celtic_cross "Celtic cross"), and others.[[h]](#cite_note-306) A common symbolism of the slanted foot stool is the foot-rest points up, toward Heaven, on Christ's right hand-side, and downward, to Hades, on Christ's left. "Between two thieves Thy Cross did prove to be a balance of righteousness: wherefore one of them was dragged down to Hades by the weight of his blasphemy *[the balance points downward]*, whereas the other was lightened of his transgressions unto the comprehension of theology *[the balance points upward]*. O Christ God, glory to Thee."[[299]](#cite_note-307)

### Local customs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Local customs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Broken_vases_on_Holy_Saturday_in_Corfu.jpg/250px-Broken_vases_on_Holy_Saturday_in_Corfu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Broken_vases_on_Holy_Saturday_in_Corfu.jpg)

Shards of pottery vases on the street, after being thrown from the windows of nearby houses. A [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") tradition in [Corfu](/wiki/Corfu "Corfu").

Locality is also expressed in regional terms of churchly jurisdiction, which is often also drawn along national lines. Many Orthodox churches adopt a national title (e.g. [Albanian Orthodox](/wiki/Albanian_Orthodox "Albanian Orthodox"), [Bulgarian Orthodox](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox "Bulgarian Orthodox"), [Antiochian Orthodox](/wiki/Antiochian_Orthodox "Antiochian Orthodox"), [Georgian Orthodox](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox "Georgian Orthodox"), [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox"), [Romanian Orthodox](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox "Romanian Orthodox"), [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox "Russian Orthodox"), [Serbian Orthodox](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox "Serbian Orthodox"), [Ukrainian Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodoxy_in_Ukraine "Orthodoxy in Ukraine"), etc.) and this title can identify which language is used in services, which bishops preside, and which of the typica is followed by specific [congregations](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/congregation "wikt:congregation"). In the Middle East, Orthodox Christians are usually referred to as *Rum* ("Roman") Orthodox, because of their historical connection with the [Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire](/wiki/Eastern_Roman_(Byzantine)_Empire "Eastern Roman (Byzantine) Empire").[[300]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBinns20023-308)

## Holy mysteries (sacraments)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Holy mysteries (sacraments)")]

The [Byzantine Rite](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite "Byzantine Rite") is used for the administration of the "Holy mysteries" or seven sacraments in [Eastern Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Christianity "Eastern Orthodox Christianity"); among these are [Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion") (the most direct connection), [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), [Chrismation](/wiki/Chrismation "Chrismation"), [confession](/wiki/Confession_(religion) "Confession (religion)"), [unction](/wiki/Unction "Unction"), [matrimony](/wiki/Marriage_in_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Marriage in the Eastern Orthodox Church"), and [ordination](/wiki/Ordination "Ordination"), as well as [blessings](/wiki/Benediction "Benediction"), [exorcisms](/wiki/Exorcism_in_Christianity "Exorcism in Christianity"), and other occasions.[[301]](#cite_note-oupbr-309)

While the Catholic Church numbers seven sacraments, and many Protestant groups list two (baptism and the Eucharist) or even none, the Eastern Orthodox do not limit the number. However, for the sake of convenience, [catechisms](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism") of the Eastern Orthodox Church will often speak of the "seven great mysteries". The term "sacrament" also properly applies to other sacred actions such as [monastic](/wiki/Eastern_Christian_monasticism "Eastern Christian monasticism") [tonsure](/wiki/Tonsure "Tonsure") or the blessing of [holy water](/wiki/Holy_water_in_Eastern_Christianity "Holy water in Eastern Christianity"), and involves [fasting](/wiki/Religious_fasting#Eastern_and_Oriental_Orthodoxy "Religious fasting"), [almsgiving](/wiki/Alms#In_Eastern_Christianity "Alms"), or an act as simple as lighting a candle, [burning incense](/wiki/Religious_use_of_incense#Christianity "Religious use of incense"), praying or asking God's blessing on food.[[302]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993274–277-310)

### Baptism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Baptism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/GreekOrthodoxBaptism1.jpg/250px-GreekOrthodoxBaptism1.jpg)](/wiki/File:GreekOrthodoxBaptism1.jpg)

An Eastern Orthodox baptism

[Baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") is the mystery which transforms the old and sinful person into a new and pure one; the old life, the sins, any mistakes made are gone and a clean slate is given. Through baptism a person is united to the [Body of Christ](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ") by becoming a member of the Eastern Orthodox Church. During the service, [water](/wiki/Holy_water_in_Eastern_Christianity "Holy water in Eastern Christianity") is blessed. The catechumen is fully immersed in the water three times in the name of the Trinity. This is considered to be a death of the "old man" by participation in the crucifixion and burial of Christ, and a rebirth into new life in Christ by participation in his resurrection.[[303]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993277–278-311)

Properly, the mystery of baptism is administered by bishops and priests; however, [in emergencies any Eastern Orthodox Christian can baptise](/wiki/Emergency_baptism "Emergency baptism").[[304]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993278-312)

### Chrismation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Chrismation")]

[Chrismation](/wiki/Chrismation "Chrismation") (sometimes called [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation")) is the mystery by which a baptised person is granted the gift of the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") through anointing with Holy [Chrism](/wiki/Chrism "Chrism").[[305]](#cite_note-313)[[306]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993278–279-314) It is normally given immediately after baptism as part of the same service, but is also used to receive lapsed members of the Eastern Orthodox Church.[[307]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarakas198756–57-315) As baptism is a person's participation in the death and resurrection of Christ, so Chrismation is a person's participation in the coming of the Holy Spirit at [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost").[[308]](#cite_note-ware1993p279-316)

A baptised and chrismated Eastern Orthodox Christian is a full member of the church and may receive the Eucharist regardless of age.[[308]](#cite_note-ware1993p279-316)

The creation of Chrism may be accomplished by any bishop at any time, but usually is done only once a year, often when a synod of bishops convenes for its annual meeting. Some autocephalous churches get their chrism from others. Anointing with it substitutes for the laying-on of hands described in the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament"), even when an instrument such as a brush is used.[[309]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarakas198757-317)

### Holy Communion (Eucharist)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Holy Communion (Eucharist)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg/250px-Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liturgy_St_James_1.jpg)

Eucharistic elements prepared for the Divine Liturgy

[Holy Communion](/wiki/Holy_Communion "Holy Communion") is given only to baptised and chrismated Eastern Orthodox Christians who have prepared by fasting, prayer and confession. The priest will administer the gifts with a spoon, called a "cochlear", directly into the recipient's mouth from the chalice.[[310]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993287-318) From baptism young infants and children are carried to the chalice to receive holy communion.[[308]](#cite_note-ware1993p279-316)

### Marriage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Marriage")]

Main article: [Marriage in the Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Marriage_in_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Marriage in the Eastern Orthodox Church")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Wedding_of_Nicholas_II_and_Alexandra_Feodorovna_by_Laurits_Tuxen_%281895-6%2C_Royal_coll.%29.jpg/330px-Wedding_of_Nicholas_II_and_Alexandra_Feodorovna_by_Laurits_Tuxen_%281895-6%2C_Royal_coll.%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wedding_of_Nicholas_II_and_Alexandra_Feodorovna_by_Laurits_Tuxen_(1895-6,_Royal_coll.).jpg)

The wedding of Tsar [Nicholas II of Russia](/wiki/Nicholas_II_of_Russia "Nicholas II of Russia") by [Laurits Tuxen](/wiki/Laurits_Tuxen "Laurits Tuxen")

From the Eastern Orthodox perspective, marriage is one of the holy mysteries or sacraments. As well as in many other Christian traditions, for example in Catholicism, it serves to unite a woman and a man in eternal union and love before God, with the purpose of following Christ and his Gospel and raising up a faithful, holy family through their holy union.[[311]](#cite_note-319)[[312]](#cite_note-320) The church understands marriage to be the union of one man and one woman, and certain Orthodox leaders have spoken out strongly in opposition to the civil institution of [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[313]](#cite_note-321)[[314]](#cite_note-322)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Greek_Orthodox_wedding_in_Tripodes%2C_Naxos%2C_119259.JPG/330px-Greek_Orthodox_wedding_in_Tripodes%2C_Naxos%2C_119259.JPG)](/wiki/File:Greek_Orthodox_wedding_in_Tripodes,_Naxos,_119259.JPG)

Greek Orthodox wedding

Jesus said that "when they rise from the dead, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven" (Mk 12:25). For the Orthodox Christian this passage should not be understood to imply that Christian marriage will not remain a reality in the Kingdom, but points to the fact that relations will not be "fleshy", but "spiritual".[[315]](#cite_note-meyendorff70-323) Love between wife and husband, as an icon of relationship between Christ and church, is eternal.[[315]](#cite_note-meyendorff70-323)

The church does recognise that there are rare occasions when it is better that couples do separate, but there is no official recognition of civil divorces. For the Eastern Orthodox, to say that marriage is indissoluble means that it should not be broken, the violation of such a union, perceived as holy, being an offence resulting from either adultery or the prolonged absence of one of the partners. Thus, permitting remarriage is an act of compassion of the church towards sinful man.[[316]](#cite_note-324)

### Holy orders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Holy orders")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Kheirotonia.jpg/250px-Kheirotonia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kheirotonia.jpg)

Eastern Orthodox [subdeacon](/wiki/Subdeacon "Subdeacon") being ordained to the [diaconate](/wiki/Diaconate "Diaconate"). The [bishop](/wiki/Bishop "Bishop") has placed his [omophorion](/wiki/Omophorion "Omophorion") and right hand on the head of the candidate and is reading the *Prayer of [Cheirotonia](/wiki/Christian_laying_on_of_hands "Christian laying on of hands")*.

Widowed priests and [deacons](/wiki/Deacon "Deacon") may not remarry and it is common for such members of the clergy to retire to a monastery (see [clerical celibacy](/wiki/Clerical_celibacy "Clerical celibacy")). This is also true of widowed wives of clergy, who do not remarry and become nuns when their children are grown. Only men are allowed to receive [holy orders](/wiki/Holy_orders "Holy orders"), although [deaconesses](/wiki/Deaconess "Deaconess") had both liturgical and pastoral functions within the church.[[317]](#cite_note-Karras-325) Women had been ordained as deacons or deaconesses in the [Byzantine Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") through the 9th century CE after which the practice fell into disuse.[[318]](#cite_note-326)[[319]](#cite_note-327)

In 2004, the Holy Synod of the [Church of Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece") voted to permit the consecration of women as 'deaconesses', without using the word 'ordination,' but only within monastic communities.[[320]](#cite_note-328) In 2016, the Holy Synod of the [Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria") voted to reinstate the [female diaconate](/wiki/Deaconess "Deaconess"); in the following year, it ordained six sub-deaconesses in the [Democratic Republic of Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_Congo "Democratic Republic of Congo"). In 2024 the Patriarchate ordained its first female deacon, Angelic Molen, in Zimbabwe, making her the first female deacon in the modern history of the Eastern Orthodox Church.[[321]](#cite_note-329)[[322]](#cite_note-330)[[323]](#cite_note-331) This move was met with criticism from other autocephalous Orthodox church leaders, such as the [Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America](/wiki/Antiochian_Orthodox_Christian_Archdiocese_of_North_America "Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America")'s [Metropolitan Saba Esber](/wiki/Saba_Esber "Saba Esber"),[[324]](#cite_note-332) and Archpriest John Whiteford of the [ROCOR](/wiki/ROCOR "ROCOR"),[[325]](#cite_note-333) who criticized the move as being politically motivated and did not accurately reflect the historical use of deaconesses in the Eastern Orthodox Church.[[326]](#cite_note-334)
