### Relations with other Christians

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Relations with other Christians")]

In 1920, the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, published an encyclical "addressed 'To all the Churches of Christ, wherever they may be', urging closer co-operation among separated Christians, and suggesting a 'League of Churches', parallel to the newly founded [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")".[[327]](#cite_note-Ware1993-335) This gesture was instrumental in the foundation of the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches") (WCC);[[328]](#cite_note-Fuchs2008-336) as such, almost all Eastern Orthodox churches are members of the WCC and "Orthodox ecclesiastics and theologians serve on its committees".[[329]](#cite_note-Benz2008-337) In 1922, the Ecumenical Patriarchate declared that the [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), [Armenian Apostolic](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church"), [Old Catholic](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"), and [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") churches possessed "the same validity" of Holy Orders and apostolic succession.[[330]](#cite_note-338)[[331]](#cite_note-339) Following the Ecumenical Patriarchate were the autocephalous churches of Alexandria, Jerusalem, Romania, and Cyprus.[[332]](#cite_note-340) However, in 1948, the Russian Orthodox Church declined to recognize the validity of Anglican Orders.[[333]](#cite_note-341) [Kallistos Ware](/wiki/Kallistos_Ware "Kallistos Ware"), a British metropolitan bishop of the Orthodox Church, has stated that [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") "is important for Orthodoxy: it has helped to force the various Orthodox churches out of their comparative isolation, making them meet one another and enter into a living contact with non-Orthodox Christians."[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWare1993322-342)

[Hilarion Alfeyev](/wiki/Hilarion_Alfeyev "Hilarion Alfeyev"), then the Metropolitan of Volokolamsk and head of external relations for the Moscow Patriarchate of the Russian Orthodox Church, stated that Orthodox and [Evangelical Protestant](/wiki/Evangelical_Protestant "Evangelical Protestant") Christians share the same positions on "such issues as [abortion](/wiki/Pro-life "Pro-life"), the [family](/wiki/Family_values#Conservative_definitions "Family values"), and [marriage](/wiki/Christian_views_on_marriage "Christian views on marriage")" and desire "vigorous grassroots engagement" between the two [Christian communions](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") on such issues.[[335]](#cite_note-Communions-343)

In that regard, the differences between the Catholic and Eastern Orthodox communions have not been improved in any relevant way. Dogmatic and [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgical "Liturgical") polarities have been significant, even and especially in recent times. A pertinent point of contention between the monarchically papal, administratively centralised Catholic Church and the decentralised confederation of Orthodox churches is the theological significance of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary").[[336]](#cite_note-344) During his visit to [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") in October 2016, [Pope Francis](/wiki/Pope_Francis "Pope Francis") was snubbed by most Orthodox Christians as he led mass before a practically empty [Mikheil Meskhi Stadium](/wiki/Mikheil_Meskhi_Stadium "Mikheil Meskhi Stadium") in [Tbilisi](/wiki/Tbilisi "Tbilisi").[[337]](#cite_note-345)

The Oriental Orthodox Churches are not in [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") with the Eastern Orthodox Church, despite their similar names. Slow dialogue towards restoring communion between the two churches began in the mid-20th century,[[338]](#cite_note-346) and, notably, in the 19th century, when the Greek Patriarch in Egypt had to absent himself from the country for a long period of time; he left his church under the guidance of the Coptic [Pope Cyril IV of Alexandria](/wiki/Pope_Cyril_IV_of_Alexandria "Pope Cyril IV of Alexandria").[[339]](#cite_note-347)

In 2019, the Primate of the [OCU](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") [Metropolitan of Kyiv and All Ukraine](/wiki/Metropolitan_of_Kyiv_and_all_Ukraine "Metropolitan of Kyiv and all Ukraine") [Epiphanius](/wiki/Epiphanius_I_of_Ukraine "Epiphanius I of Ukraine") stated that "theoretically" the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") and the [Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church](/wiki/Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") could in the future unite into a united church around the Kyiv throne.[[340]](#cite_note-348) In 2019, the Primate of the UGCC, [Major Archbishop of Kyiv-Galicia](/wiki/Lists_of_leaders_of_the_Ukrainian_Greek_Catholic_Church "Lists of leaders of the Ukrainian Greek Catholic Church") [Sviatoslav](/wiki/Sviatoslav_Shevchuk "Sviatoslav Shevchuk"), stated that every effort should be made to restore the original unity of the Kyivan Church in its Orthodox and Catholic branches, saying that the restoration of Eucharistic communion between [Rome](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") and [Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") is not a utopia.[[341]](#cite_note-349)

Notwithstanding certain overtures by both Catholic and Eastern Orthodox leaders, the majority of Orthodox Christians, as well as Catholics, are not in favour of communion between their churches, with only a median of 35 per cent and 38 per cent, respectively, claiming support.[[141]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-141)

### Relations with Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Relations with Islam")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/1821_atrocities_Constantinople.jpg/250px-1821_atrocities_Constantinople.jpg)](/wiki/File:1821_atrocities_Constantinople.jpg)

The [Constantinople Massacre of April 1821](/wiki/Constantinople_Massacre_of_1821 "Constantinople Massacre of 1821"): a religious persecution of the Greek population of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") under the Ottomans. Patriarch [Gregory V of Constantinople](/wiki/Gregory_V_of_Constantinople "Gregory V of Constantinople") was executed.

In 2007, Metropolitan [Alfeyev](/wiki/Hilarion_Alfeyev "Hilarion Alfeyev") expressed the possibility of peaceful coexistence between Islam and [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") in Russia, as the two religions have never had religious wars in Russia.[[342]](#cite_note-350)

## Constituencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Constituencies")]

See also: [List of the current autocephalous Eastern Orthodox churches and their dependencies](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church_organization "Eastern Orthodox Church organization")

The various [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous") and autonomous [synods](/wiki/Synods "Synods") of the Eastern Orthodox Church are distinct in terms of administration and local culture, but for the most part exist in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with one another. However, there have been varying instances in the history of the Eastern Orthodox Church where communion has been broken between member churches for short periods, particularly over autocephaly issues or disagreements over [ecumenism](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") with other Christian denominations.[[343]](#cite_note-351)[[344]](#cite_note-352)[[345]](#cite_note-353)[[346]](#cite_note-354) In addition, some schismatic churches not in any communion exist, with all three groups identifying as Eastern Orthodox.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/%CE%A0%CE%B1%CF%81%CE%BF%CF%85%CF%83%CE%AF%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%85_%CE%A5%CF%80%CE%BF%CF%85%CF%81%CE%B3%CE%BF%CF%8D_%CE%95%CE%BE%CF%89%CF%84%CE%B5%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BA%CF%8E%CE%BD_%CE%9D._%CE%9A%CE%BF%CF%84%CE%B6%CE%B9%CE%AC_%CF%83%CF%84%CE%B7%CE%BD_%CE%91%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1_%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%B9_%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%B3%CE%AC%CE%BB%CE%B7_%CE%A3%CF%8D%CE%BD%CE%BF%CE%B4%CE%BF_%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%9F%CF%81%CE%B8%CF%8C%CE%B4%CE%BF%CE%BE%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%95%CE%BA%CE%BA%CE%BB%CE%B7%CF%83%CE%AF%CE%B1%CF%82%2C_%CE%9A%CF%81%CE%AE%CF%84%CE%B7%2C_16-27-6-2016_%2827614224782%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:%CE%A0%CE%B1%CF%81%CE%BF%CF%85%CF%83%CE%AF%CE%B1_%CF%84%CE%BF%CF%85_%CE%A5%CF%80%CE%BF%CF%85%CF%81%CE%B3%CE%BF%CF%8D_%CE%95%CE%BE%CF%89%CF%84%CE%B5%CF%81%CE%B9%CE%BA%CF%8E%CE%BD_%CE%9D._%CE%9A%CE%BF%CF%84%CE%B6%CE%B9%CE%AC_%CF%83%CF%84%CE%B7%CE%BD_%CE%91%CE%B3%CE%AF%CE%B1_%CE%BA%CE%B1%CE%B9_%CE%9C%CE%B5%CE%B3%CE%AC%CE%BB%CE%B7_%CE%A3%CF%8D%CE%BD%CE%BF%CE%B4%CE%BF_%CF%84%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%9F%CF%81%CE%B8%CF%8C%CE%B4%CE%BF%CE%BE%CE%B7%CF%82_%CE%95%CE%BA%CE%BA%CE%BB%CE%B7%CF%83%CE%AF%CE%B1%CF%82,_%CE%9A%CF%81%CE%AE%CF%84%CE%B7,_16-27-6-2016_(27614224782).jpg)

The [Pan-Orthodox Council](/wiki/Pan-Orthodox_Council "Pan-Orthodox Council"), Kolymvari, Crete, Greece, June 2016

Another group of non-mainstream Eastern Orthodox Christians are referred [True Orthodoxy](/wiki/True_Orthodoxy "True Orthodoxy") or [Old Calendarists](/wiki/Old_Calendarists "Old Calendarists"); they are those who, without authority from their parent churches, have continued to use the old [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), and split from their parent church.[[347]](#cite_note-:13-355)

The [Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church_Outside_Russia "Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia") (ROCOR) has [united in 2007 with the Moscow Patriarchate](/wiki/Act_of_Canonical_Communion_with_the_Moscow_Patriarchate "Act of Canonical Communion with the Moscow Patriarchate"); these two churches had separated from each other in the 1920s due to the subjection of the latter to the hostile [Soviet regime](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union").[[348]](#cite_note-356)

Another group, called the [Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers"), [separated in 1666](/wiki/Raskol "Raskol") from the official Russian Orthodox Church as a protest against church rite reforms introduced by [Patriarch Nikon of Moscow](/wiki/Patriarch_Nikon_of_Moscow "Patriarch Nikon of Moscow").[[349]](#cite_note-357)

### Main communion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Main communion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/AlexGreekCathedralFront.jpg/250px-AlexGreekCathedralFront.jpg)](/wiki/File:AlexGreekCathedralFront.jpg)

[Cathedral of Evangelismos](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Evangelismos "Cathedral of Evangelismos"), [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria")

The Eastern Orthodox Church is a [communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") of 15 [autocephalous](/wiki/Autocephalous "Autocephalous")—that is, administratively completely independent—regional churches,[[350]](#cite_note-358) plus the [Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America") and two [Ukrainian](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") [Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_(Moscow_Patriarchate) "Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)"). The Orthodox Church in America is recognised as autocephalous only by the Russian, Bulgarian, Georgian, Polish and Czech-Slovak churches. In December 2018, representatives of two unrecognised Ukrainian Orthodox churches, along with two [metropolitans](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") of the recognised, but self-declared autocephalous [Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_of_the_Moscow_Patriarchate "Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate"), [proclaimed the formation of the unified Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Unification_council_of_the_Eastern_Orthodox_churches_of_Ukraine "Unification council of the Eastern Orthodox churches of Ukraine"). On 5 January 2019, the Orthodox Church of Ukraine received its *[tomos](/wiki/Tomos_(Eastern_Orthodox_Church) "Tomos (Eastern Orthodox Church)")* of autocephaly (decree which defines the conditions of a church's independence) from the Ecumenical Patriarchate and thus received a place in the [diptych](/wiki/Diptych#Ecclesiastical "Diptych").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Manastir_Pecka_Patrijarsija_2.jpg/250px-Manastir_Pecka_Patrijarsija_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manastir_Pecka_Patrijarsija_2.jpg)

[Patriarchate of Peć](/wiki/Patriarchate_of_Pe%C4%87_(monastery) "Patriarchate of Peć (monastery)") in [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo"), the seat of the [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") from the 14th century, when its status was upgraded into a patriarchate

Each church has defined geographical boundaries of its jurisdiction and is ruled by its council of bishops or synod presided by a senior bishop–its [primate](/wiki/Primate_(bishop) "Primate (bishop)") (or first hierarch). The primate may carry the honorary title of patriarch, metropolitan (in the Slavic tradition) or archbishop (in the Greek tradition).

Each regional church consists of constituent [eparchies](/wiki/Eparchies "Eparchies") (or dioceses) ruled by a bishop. Some churches have given an eparchy or group of eparchies varying degrees of [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy_(Eastern_Orthodoxy) "Autonomy (Eastern Orthodoxy)") (self-government). Such autonomous churches maintain varying levels of dependence on their [mother church](/wiki/Mother_church "Mother church"), usually defined in a [*tomos*](/wiki/Tomos_(Eastern_Orthodox_Church) "Tomos (Eastern Orthodox Church)") or other document of autonomy.

Below is a list of the 15 autocephalous Orthodox churches forming the main body of Orthodox Christianity, all of which are titled equal to each other, but the Ecumenical Patriarchate is titled the *first among equals*. Based on the definitions, the list is in the order of precedence and alphabetical order where necessary, with some of their constituent autonomous churches and exarchates listed as well. The liturgical title of the primate is in italics.

* [Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarchate_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople") (*Archbishop of Constantinople-New Rome and First Among Equals Patriarch*)
  + Autonomous [Orthodox Church of Finland](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Finland "Orthodox Church of Finland") (*Archbishop of Helsinki and All Finland*, formerly *Archbishop of Karelia and All Finland*)
  + Self-governing Orthodox [Church of Crete](/wiki/Church_of_Crete "Church of Crete") (*Archbishop of Crete*)
  + Self-governing [monastic community of Mount Athos](/wiki/Monastic_community_of_Mount_Athos "Monastic community of Mount Athos")
  + Self-governing [Orthodox Church of Korea](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Korea "Orthodox Church of Korea") (*Metropolitan of Seoul and All Korea*)
* [Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Alexandria "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Alexandria") (*the Pope and Patriarch of the Great City of [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya"), [Pentapolis](/wiki/Pentapolis_(North_Africa) "Pentapolis (North Africa)"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), all the land of [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), and all Africa*)
* [Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Antioch](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Antioch "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Antioch") (*Patriarch of Antioch and all the East*)
  + Self-governing [Archdiocese of North America](/wiki/Antiochian_Orthodox_Christian_Archdiocese_of_North_America "Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese of North America") (Metropolitan of North America and Archbishop of New York)
* [Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Patriarchate_of_Jerusalem "Greek Orthodox Patriarchate of Jerusalem") (*Patriarch of the Holy City of Jerusalem and all Holy Land, Syria, Arabia, beyond the Jordan River, Cana of Galilee, and Sacred Zion*)
  + Autonomous [Church of Mount Sinai](/wiki/Church_of_Sinai "Church of Sinai") (*Archbishop of Choreb, Sinai, and Raitha*)
* [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") (*Patriarch of Moscow and all Russia*)
  + Autonomous [Orthodox Church in Japan](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_Japan "Orthodox Church in Japan") (*Archbishop of Tokyo and Metropolitan of All Japan*)
  + [Exarchate of Belarus](/wiki/Belarusian_Orthodox_Church "Belarusian Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Minsk and Slutsk, Patriarchal Exarch of All Belarus*)
  + Self-governing [Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church_Outside_Russia "Russian Orthodox Church Outside Russia") (*Metropolitan of Eastern America and New York, First Hierarch of the Russian church abroad*)
* [Serbian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Serbian_Orthodox_Church "Serbian Orthodox Church") (*Archbishop of Peć, Metropolitan of Belgrade and Karlovci, and Serbian Patriarch*)
* [Bulgarian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Bulgarian_Orthodox_Church "Bulgarian Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Sofia and Patriarch of All Bulgaria*)
* [Romanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church") (*Archbishop of Bucharest, Metropolitan of Muntenia and Dobrudja, Locum Tenens of the Throne of Caesarea of Cappadocia, and Patriarch of Romania*)
  + Autonomous [Romanian Orthodox Metropolis of the Americas](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Metropolis_of_the_Americas "Romanian Orthodox Metropolis of the Americas") (*Romanian Orthodox Archbishop of the United States of America and Romanian Orthodox Metropolitan of the Americas*)
* [Georgian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Georgian_Orthodox_Church "Georgian Orthodox Church") (*Catholicos-Patriarch of All Georgia, the Archbishop of Mtskheta-Tbilisi and Metropolitan bishop of Abkhazia and Pitsunda*)
* [Church of Cyprus](/wiki/Church_of_Cyprus "Church of Cyprus") (*Archbishop of New Justiniana and all Cyprus*)
* [Church of Greece](/wiki/Church_of_Greece "Church of Greece") (*Archbishop of Athens and all Greece*)
* [Albanian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Albanian_Orthodox_Church "Albanian Orthodox Church") (*Archbishop of Tirana, Durres and all Albania*)
* [Polish Orthodox Church](/wiki/Polish_Orthodox_Church "Polish Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Warsaw and all Poland* or *Archbishop of Warsaw and Metropolitan of All Poland)[[i]](#cite_note-360)*
* [Orthodox Church of the Czech Lands and Slovakia](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_the_Czech_Lands_and_Slovakia "Orthodox Church of the Czech Lands and Slovakia") (*Archbishop of Prague, the Metropolitan of Czech lands and Slovakia or the Archbishop of Presov, the Metropolitan of Czech lands and Slovakia*)
* [Macedonian Orthodox Church – Ohrid Archbishopric](/wiki/Macedonian_Orthodox_Church_%E2%80%93_Ohrid_Archbishopric "Macedonian Orthodox Church – Ohrid Archbishopric") (*Metropolitan of Skopje and Archbishop of Ohrid and Macedonia and of Justiniana Prima*)

Within the main body of Eastern Orthodoxy there are unresolved internal issues as to the autonomous or autocephalous status or legitimacy of the following Orthodox churches, particularly between those stemming from the Russian Orthodox or Constantinopolitan churches:

* [Orthodox Church in America](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_in_America "Orthodox Church in America") (*Archbishop of Washington, Metropolitan of All America and Canada*) – Autocephaly not recognised by the Ecumenical Patriarchate.
* Self-governing [Estonian Apostolic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Estonian_Apostolic_Orthodox_Church "Estonian Apostolic Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Tallinn and all Estonia*) – Recognised only by the Ecumenical Patriarchate, opposed only by the Russian Orthodox Church.
* Self-governing [Estonian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate](/wiki/Estonian_Orthodox_Church_of_the_Moscow_Patriarchate "Estonian Orthodox Church of the Moscow Patriarchate") (*Metropolitan of Tallinn and all Estonia*) – Declared itself autocephalous in 2024; not recognised by the Ecumenical Patriarchate.
* Autonomous [Bessarabian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Bessarabian_Orthodox_Church "Bessarabian Orthodox Church") in Moldova (*Archbishop of Chișinău, Metropolitan of Bessarabia and Exarch of the Territories*) of the Romanian Orthodox Church – Territory claimed by the Russian Orthodox Church.
* Autonomous [Moldovan Orthodox Church](/wiki/Moldovan_Orthodox_Church "Moldovan Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Chișinău and all Moldova*) of the Russian Orthodox Church – Jurisdiction disputed by the Romanian Orthodox Church.
* [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine") (*Metropolitan of Kyiv and All Ukraine*) – Recognised by the Ecumenical Patriarchate, Church of Greece, Church of Cyprus, and Patriarchate of Alexandria[[352]](#cite_note-361)[[353]](#cite_note-362)[[354]](#cite_note-363)[[355]](#cite_note-364)[[356]](#cite_note-365)[[357]](#cite_note-366) as of October 2020, opposed by the Russian, Antiochian, Czech and Slovak, Serbian and Polish Orthodox Churches, and the Orthodox Church in America.[[358]](#cite_note-367)[[359]](#cite_note-368)[[360]](#cite_note-369)
* [Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)](/wiki/Ukrainian_Orthodox_Church_(Moscow_Patriarchate) "Ukrainian Orthodox Church (Moscow Patriarchate)"), self-governing by declaration which later was approved and recognised by the Georgian Orthodox Church[[361]](#cite_note-370) – jurisdiction disputed with the [Orthodox Church of Ukraine](/wiki/Orthodox_Church_of_Ukraine "Orthodox Church of Ukraine"), which nearly all Churches continued to recognise as part of ROC.
* [Latvian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Latvian_Orthodox_Church "Latvian Orthodox Church") (*Metropolitan of Riga and all Latvia*) – holding autocephaly prior to 1941, forcibly integrated in 1941 as a result of the [Soviet occupation and annexation of the Baltic states](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_of_the_Baltic_states_(1940) "Soviet occupation of the Baltic states (1940)") to become a self-governing part of the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church"), with the Ecumenical Patriarchate accepting this situation in 1978; in 2022, the Latvian Parliament (the [Saeima](/wiki/Saeima "Saeima")) declared the restoration of autocephaly of the LOC from the ROC, due to security reasons.

### Traditionalist groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Traditionalist groups")]

#### True Orthodox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: True Orthodox")]

Main article: [Eastern Orthodox Church organization § True Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church_organization#True_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox Church organization")

[True Orthodoxy](/wiki/True_Orthodoxy "True Orthodoxy") has been separated from the mainstream communion over issues of ecumenism and calendar reform since the 1920s.[[362]](#cite_note-politika2-371) The movement rejects the Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople, the Moscow Patriarchate, and all churches which are in communion with them, accusing them of heresy and placing themselves under bishops who do the same thing. They adhere to the use of the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"), claiming that the [calendar reform in the 1920s](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar") is in contradiction with the ecumenical councils. There is no official communion of True Orthodox; and they often are local groups and are limited to a specific bishop or locality.

#### Old calendarists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Old calendarists")]

Main article: [Old Calendarists](/wiki/Old_Calendarists "Old Calendarists")

[Old Calendarists](/wiki/Old_Calendarists "Old Calendarists") are traditionalist groups of [Eastern Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Christians "Eastern Orthodox Christians") that separated from mainstream Eastern Orthodox churches because [some of the latter](/wiki/New_Calendarists "New Calendarists") [adopted](/wiki/Council_of_Constantinople_(1923) "Council of Constantinople (1923)") the [revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar") while Old Calendarists remained committed to the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar").*[[363]](#cite_note-:0-372)[[347]](#cite_note-:13-355)* Old Calendarists are not in [communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with any mainstream Eastern Orthodox churches.[[364]](#cite_note-373) "Old Calendarists" is another name for the [True Orthodox](/wiki/True_Orthodox "True Orthodox") movement in [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") and [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus").[[365]](#cite_note-:12-374) In 1999, it was estimated that "[t]here are probably over one million Old Calendarists in Romania, somewhat fewer in Greece, and considerably fewer in [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), and the Eastern Orthodox [diaspora](/wiki/Diaspora "Diaspora")."[[366]](#cite_note-:2-375)

#### Old Believers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Old Believers")]

Main article: [Old Believers § Old Believer churches](/wiki/Old_Believers#Old_Believer_churches "Old Believers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Crucession_Davidovo-Elizarovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8504.jpg/250px-Crucession_Davidovo-Elizarovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8504.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crucession_Davidovo-Elizarovo_Guslitci_Moscow_reg_8504.jpg)

Traditional [Paschal](/wiki/Easter "Easter") procession by [Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Old-Rite_Church "Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church")

[Old Believers](/wiki/Old_Believers "Old Believers") are groups which do not accept the liturgical reforms which were carried out within the Russian Orthodox Church by [Patriarch Nikon of Moscow](/wiki/Patriarch_Nikon_of_Moscow "Patriarch Nikon of Moscow") in the 17th century. Although all of the groups of Old Believers emerged as a result of opposition to the Nikonian reforms, they do not constitute a single monolithic body. Despite their emphasis on invariable adherence to the pre-Nikonian traditions, the Old Believers feature a great diversity of groups which profess different interpretations of church tradition and they are often not in communion with each other (some groups even practise re-baptism before admitting a member of another group into their midst).

### Churches not in communion with other churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Eastern_Orthodox_Church&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Churches not in communion with other churches")]

Main article: [Organization of the Eastern Orthodox Church § Churches that are not recognised despite wanting to](/wiki/Organization_of_the_Eastern_Orthodox_Church#Churches_that_are_not_recognised_despite_wanting_to "Organization of the Eastern Orthodox Church")

Churches existing with irregular or unresolved canonical status are entities that have carried out [episcopal consecrations outside of the norms of canon law](/wiki/Episcopi_vagantes "Episcopi vagantes"), or whose bishops have been excommunicated by one of the mainstream autocephalous churches. These include nationalist and other schismatic bodies such as the [Abkhazian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Abkhazian_Orthodox_Church "Abkhazian Orthodox Church") and [American Orthodox Catholic Church](/wiki/American_Orthodox_Catholic_Church "American Orthodox Catholic Church").

This term refers to the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), in contrast to the more recent (15th century) term "Russia". See also: [Names of Rus', Russia and Ruthenia](/wiki/Names_of_Rus%27,_Russia_and_Ruthenia "Names of Rus', Russia and Ruthenia").

Lunkin said that this was long known by experts but a myth persists that 80–90% of the population is Orthodox.[[209]](#cite_note-211) According to *The World Factbook* 2006 estimate, 15–20% are practising Russian Orthodox but there is a large populations of non-practising believers.[[210]](#cite_note-CIAWeb-212)

"In 1928 a second group of Ukrainian Orthodox faithful in the USA initiated a movement toward Orthodoxy. Because of the questions surrounding the status of Archbishop John, the group hesitated in affiliating itself with his already established jurisdiction even though it was thriving. The first Sobor of this group met in Allentown, PA in the spring of 1929 and established itself as the Ukrainian Orthodox Church in America. Rev. Dr. Joseph Zuk was elected as administrator to organize the diocese and at its second Sobor of 1931 in New York City he was elected as its first Bishop. Two hierarchs of the Syrian Orthodox Church in the USA consecrated Bishop Zuk in 1932 at St. Volodymyr Cathedral, on 14th Street in New York City. Unfortunately, Bishop Joseph lived less than two years following his consecration."

"Patriarch Bartholomew issued an 'Act' on 20 February 1996 on the renewal of the 1923 Tomos of Patriarch Meletius IV and on the establishment of the 'Autonomous Orthodox Estonian Metropolia' on the territory of Estonia. Temporal administration was entrusted to Archbishop John of Karelia and All Finland. A schismatic group headed by the suspended clergymen was accepted into canonical communion. Thus the schism in Estonia became a reality.  
       
     On 23 February 1996, in response to the one-sided and illegal actions of Patriarch Bartholomew the Holy Synod of the Russian Orthodox Church resolved to recognise them 'as schismatic and compelling our Church to suspend canonical and Eucharistic communion with the Patriarchate of Constantinople… and to omit the name of the Patriarch of Constantinople in the diptych of the Primates of the Local Orthodox Churches.'"

"To admit into communion schismatics and a person anathematized in other Local Church with all the 'bishops' and 'clergy' consecrated by him, the encroachment on somebody else's canonical regions, the attempt to abandon its own historical decisions and commitments – all this leads the Patriarchate of Constantinople beyond the canonical space and, to our great grief, makes it impossible for us to continue the Eucharistic community with its hierarch, clergy and laity. Francis Xavier (27 Oct. 1872–12 July 1960), RC Bishop of Darwin, 1938–49; Titular Bishop of Paris; assistant at the Pontifical throne, Rome, 1951", *Who Was Who*, Oxford University Press, 1 December 2007, [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1093/ww/9780199540884.013.u238027](https://doi.org/10.1093%2Fww%2F9780199540884.013.u238027)
     "Everywhere following the decrees of the Holy Fathers, and aware of the recently recognized Canon of the one hundred and fifty most God-beloved Bishops who convened during the reign of Theodosius the Great of pious memory, who became emperor in the imperial city of Constantinople otherwise known as New Rome; we too decree and vote the same things in regard to the privileges and priorities of the most holy Church of that same Constantinople and New Rome. And this is in keeping with the fact that the Fathers naturally enough granted the priorities to the throne of Old Rome on account of her being the imperial capital. And motivated by the same object and aim the one hundred and fifty most God-beloved Bishops have accorded the like priorities to the most holy throne of New Rome, with good reason deeming that the city which is the seat of an empire, and of a senate, and is equal to old imperial Rome in respect of other privileges and priorities, should be magnified also as she is in respect of ecclesiastical affairs, as coming next after her, or as being second to her."

327. **[^](#cite_ref-Ware1993_335-0)** [Ware 1993](#CITEREFWare1993), p. 322 "From the beginning of the twentieth century the Ecumenical Patriarchate has shown a special concern for Christian reconciliation. At his accession in 1902, Patriarch Joachim III sent an encyclical letter to all the autocephalous Orthodox Churches, asking in particular for their opinion on relations with other Christian bodies. In January 1920 the Ecumenical Patriarchate followed this up with a bold and prophetic letter addressed 'To all the Churches of Christ, wherever they may be', urging closer co-operation among separated Christians, and suggesting a 'League of Churches', parallel to the newly founded League of Nations. Many of the ideas in this letter anticipate subsequent developments in the WCC. Constantinople, along with several of the other Orthodox Churches, was represented at the Faith and Order Conferences at Lausanne in 1927 and at Edinburgh in 1937. The Ecumenical Patriarchate also participated in the first Assembly of the WCC at Amsterdam in 1948, and has been a consistent supporter of the work of the WCC ever since."
